evidence_citations.excerpt: I feel like I’m losing myself and everyone’s expectations are crushing me. I keep wondering if I’m even good enough to keep going.
I keep thinking my worth is tied to grades and my part‑time job, and that makes me feel like I’m not enough.

I tried a 5‑minute breathing exercise after class, it helped a bit, but the argument with my parents keeps replaying in my head. I’m also anxious about whether the money I’m putting into my part‑time job will cover my tuition, and the thought that I might fail makes me feel not enough.

persona_updates.new_value: overthinker, emotionally reactive, avoidant, perfectionistic, people-pleaser, fearful, self-doubting, anxious

evidence_citations.excerpt: I feel like I’m losing myself and everyone’s expectations are crushing me. I keep wondering if I’m even good enough to keep going.
I keep thinking my worth is tied to grades and my part‑time job, and that makes me feel like I’m not enough.

I tried a 5‑minute breathing exercise after class, it helped a bit, but the argument with my parents keeps replaying in my head. I’m also anxious about whether the money I’m putting into my part‑time job will cover my tuition, and the thought that I might fail makes me feel not enough.

transcript.text: I don't know how to handle the fight with my parents, and now I have to juggle midterms and a part‑time job to pay for school. I feel like I’m losing myself and everyone’s expectations are crushing me. I keep wondering if I’m even good enough to keep going.

You're dealing with a lot right now, from the fight with your parents to the pressure of midterms and a part-time job. It sounds like the weight of everyone's expectations, including your own, is really getting to you. You mentioned feeling like you're losing yourself and wondering if you're good enough to keep going. That takes a lot of courage to admit. Can you tell me more about what's been the hardest part of dealing with these expectations?

The hardest part is feeling like I'm walking on a tightrope—my parents are threatening to pull the rope, my job is draining me, and every time I look at my grades I feel like I'm not enough. I keep worrying that if I fail I’ll lose everything.

The image of walking on a tightrope really captures the precariousness you're feeling, with so many things that could go wrong and make you feel like you're not enough. It's like every aspect of your life is heightened and sensitive right now. Your parents' threats, the drain of your job, and the pressure of your grades are all compounding to make you feel this way. Can you think of any moments, no matter how small, where you've managed to find some balance or relief from this tightrope feeling?

I went for a run after class and found the rhythm of my feet a tiny anchor amid the chaos, but the argument with my parents still gnaws at me. I keep thinking my worth is tied to grades and my part‑time job, and that makes me feel like I’m not enough. Even when I’m in the library, the hum of other people feels like a reminder that I’m not fitting in.

That run after class sounds like it was a really intentional way to clear your head and find some calm. The fact that the rhythm of your feet could be an anchor, even if just for a little while, says a lot about your ability to find small moments of peace. It's like you're recognizing that you don't have to be defined by the chaos around you, including the argument with your parents and the pressure of grades and your part-time job. Yeah, it's tough to shake off the feeling that your worth is tied to all those things, but it's interesting that you mention the hum of other people in the library feeling like a reminder that you're not fitting in - it's like you're feeling the weight of everyone's expectations, including your own.

The fight with my parents still gnaws at me, and I keep replaying it in my head, making me feel like I’m not enough. I’m also worried about the money I’m putting into the job and whether I can keep paying for school. On top of that, I feel anxious in the library, like everyone is watching and judging me.

It's really tough to feel like you're being watched and judged all the time, especially in a place that's supposed to be a safe space for learning like the library. The weight of your parents' expectations, combined with the pressure of your job and grades, is a heavy burden. I want you to know that I see you, and I see how hard you're trying to keep everything together. That run you went on after class, finding calm in the rhythm of your feet, shows me that you have the ability to find peace, even if it's just for a moment. Can you think of any other small moments or activities that have helped you feel more grounded and less anxious?

I tried a 5‑minute breathing exercise after class, it helped a bit, but the argument with my parents keeps replaying in my head. I’m also anxious about whether the money I’m putting into my part‑time job will cover my tuition, and the thought that I might fail makes me feel not enough. The library feels like a spotlight, and I keep feeling watched and judged.

I want to acknowledge the effort you put into trying that 5-minute breathing exercise after class - it takes a lot of courage to try and find calm in the midst of so much stress. Even though the argument with your parents and the anxiety about your part-time job and tuition are still weighing on you, the fact that the breathing exercise helped 'a bit' suggests that you're on the right track. Can you tell me more about what you felt during those 5 minutes, and how you think you could build on that sense of calm in other moments?

I did feel a little calmer when I stopped my breathing, but then I imagined my parents yelling again and the relief vanished. I keep thinking about whether the money from my job will cover tuition, and that keeps the anxiety at the back of my mind. Maybe I need to find a way to keep that calm longer, but right now it feels like a fleeting spark.

You had a moment of calm during that breathing exercise, but then the image of your parents yelling and the worry about tuition payments crept back in, making that calm feeling disappear. It's like those worries are always lurking, ready to disrupt any sense of peace you find. Yeah, it can be really tough to hold onto that calm when it feels like everything else is telling you that you're not enough. What do you think might help you extend that calm, even if it's just for a little while longer?

I keep thinking about how I could keep that calm, but every time I try, the memory of my parents yelling and the worry about whether the job will cover tuition pops back up. It feels like a constant reminder that I’m not enough, especially when I’m in the library and everyone seems to be watching me.

It's really tough to hold onto that calm when the memories of your parents yelling and the worry about tuition payments keep popping back up. I want to acknowledge the effort you put into trying to find calm, even if it feels fleeting. It takes a lot of courage to try and find peace in the midst of so much stress. The fact that you've tried breathing exercises and found some calm, even if it was just for a moment, suggests that you're on the right track. What do you think might help you extend that calm, even if it's just for a little while longer?

I keep thinking about how to keep that calm, but the memory of my parents yelling and the worry about whether my part‑time job will cover tuition keep popping up. It feels like a constant reminder that I’m not enough, especially in the library where everyone seems to be watching. I’m not sure if I can even hold onto that calm for a minute longer.

The weight of your parents' expectations and the pressure to cover tuition with your part-time job is really bearing down on you, making it feel like you're not enough, especially when you're in the library and it feels like everyone's watching. It's like those worries are always lurking, ready to disrupt any sense of peace you find. I want to acknowledge how hard it is to hold onto calm when it feels like everything is telling you that you're not doing enough. Yeah, it's tough to shake off that feeling, but I'm here to support you, and I want you to know that I see you trying to find ways to cope, even if it's just for a minute longer.

I keep replaying the argument, and it feels like nothing changes. The worry about whether the job pays enough for tuition keeps me awake, and I feel like I'm failing. In the library, I feel like everyone is watching and judging me.

You're still carrying the weight of that argument with your parents, and the worry about your job and tuition is keeping you up at night. It's like you're stuck in a loop, and it feels like nothing is changing. I want to acknowledge how hard it is to shake off that feeling of not being enough, especially when it feels like everyone is watching and judging you in the library. Yeah, it's tough to find calm when it feels like everything is telling you that you're not doing enough. What's one thing that you've done recently that's helped you feel even slightly more grounded, even if it was just for a moment?

I did try writing a short poem after class, but the memory of my parents yelling still lingers and I keep thinking I’m not enough. I also keep worrying if the money from my part‑time job will cover tuition, and the library feels like a spotlight that makes me feel judged.

I can see that writing a poem after class was an attempt to express yourself and find some calm, and I want to acknowledge the courage it takes to try and find peace in the midst of so much stress. It's like the memory of your parents yelling and the worry about tuition are always lurking, ready to disrupt any sense of peace you find. Yeah, it's tough to shake off that feeling of not being enough, especially when it feels like everyone is watching and judging you in the library. What did you write about in that poem, and how did it make you feel?
